seo.title: Building LoRA and parameter-efficient fine-tuning with Op...
seo.description: A step-by-step implementation guide for LoRA and parameter-efficient fine-tuning with OpenAI Fine-Tuning API and Hugging Face.

content.intro: This guide provides a structured approach to fine-tuning foundation models for domain-specific tasks, focusing on dataset preparation, model selection, evaluation, and deployment. Each step includes actionable checks and integration notes for production workflows.
prerequisites: Dataset in JSONL or CSV format
Access to model hub (e.g., Hugging Face, OpenAI)

steps.title: Prepare and validate training data
steps.explanation: Clean data by removing duplicates, irrelevant content, and inconsistent formatting. Split into training/validation/test sets (80/10/10). Use pandas or datasets library for validation. Ensure domain-specific edge cases are included.

code.content:
from datasets import load_dataset
raw_data = load_dataset('json', data_files='training.jsonl')
print(raw_data['train'][:5])

pitfalls: Ignoring class imbalance in labeled datasets
Using unverified data sources without sanitization

steps.title: Select fine-tuning approach and base model
steps.explanation: Choose between full fine-tuning, LoRA, or QLoRA based on hardware constraints. Select a base model from Hugging Face that matches your task (e.g., 'meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-hf' for general tasks). Document model card metadata.
pitfalls: Selecting models without compatible inference frameworks

steps.title: Configure training parameters
steps.explanation: Set learning rate (2e-5 to 5e-5), batch size (per GPU 1-4), and epochs (3-10). Use W&B or MLflow for experiment tracking. Define evaluation metrics (e.g., perplexity, accuracy) in trainer configuration.

code.content:
from transformers import TrainingArguments
training_args = TrainingArguments(
    output_dir='./results',
    learning_rate=2e-5,
    per_device_train_batch_size=4,
    num_train_epochs=5,
    evaluation_strategy='epoch'
)

pitfalls: Using default parameters without domain adaptation
Ignoring gradient checkpointing for memory optimization

steps.title: Execute training with parameter-efficient methods
steps.explanation: Implement LoRA/QLoRA with peft library. Monitor loss curves and validation metrics. Save checkpoints at regular intervals. Use mixed precision training where supported.

code.content:
from peft import LoraConfig
lora_config = LoraConfig(
    r=64,
    lora_alpha=128,
    lora_dropout=0.1,
    task_type='CAUSAL_LM'
)

pitfalls: Failing to freeze base model weights correctly
Not validating memory usage during training

steps.title: Evaluate and compare model versions
steps.explanation: Test against baseline models using standardized benchmarks. Measure inference latency and token throughput. Use model card templates to document performance metrics and limitations.

code.content:
from evaluate import load
metric = load('accuracy')
results = metric.compute(predictions=outputs, references=labels)
print(results)

pitfalls: Evaluating on training data without fresh test sets
Ignoring latency requirements for production use

steps.title: Optimize for production deployment
steps.explanation: Quantize models using transformers' quantization tools. Containerize with Docker. Use vLLM or Ollama for efficient serving. Set up health checks and auto-scaling configurations.

code.content:
ollama create my-model -f ./model.tar.gz
ollama run my-model

pitfalls: Deploying unquantized models to GPU instances

content.conclusion: Successful fine-tuning requires rigorous data validation, parameter optimization, and production-aware deployment. Regularly audit model performance and update training pipelines to maintain quality as data distributions shift.
